coredata.dc:title: Fentogram electrochemical detection of HIV RNA based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Moço, Anna C.R.
Gomide, José A.L.
Flauzino, Jose M.R.
Brussasco, Jéssica G.
Luz, Luiz F.G.
Soares, Márcia M.C.N.
Madurro, João M.
Brito-Madurro, Ana G.
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the construction of an HIV-specific genosensor through the modification of carbon screen-printed electrodes (CSPE) with graphene quantum dots decorated with L-cysteine and gold nanoparticles (cys-GQDs/AuNps). Cys-GQDs were characterized by FT-IR and UV–vis spectra and electronic properties of the modified electrodes were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The modification of the electrode surface with cys-GQDs and AuNps increased the electrochemical performance of the electrode, improving the electron transfer of the anionic redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- on the electrochemical platform. When compared to the bare surface, the modified electrode showed a 1.7 times increase in effective electrode area and a 29 times decrease in charge transfer resistance. The genosensor response was performed by differential pulse voltammetry, monitoring the current response of the anionic redox probe, confirmed with real genomic RNA samples, making it possible to detect 1 fg/mL. In addition, the genosensor maintained its response for 60 days at room temperature. This new genosensor platform for early detection of HIV, based on the modification of the electrode surface with cys-GQDs and AuNps, discriminates between HIV-negative and positive samples, showing a low detection limit, as well as good specificity and stability, which are relevant properties for commercial application of biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical biosensor
Human immunodeficiency virus
Carbon quantum dots
Nanomaterials
RNA detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Genosensor assembly (CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps/HIV1S) and electrochemical detection of HIV RNA
Characterization of cys-GQDs by FT-IR and UV–vis spectra
Functionalization and Detection of the HIV RNA
Calibration curve, storage stability and analysis with biological samples for HIV

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface modification with cys-GQDs (A) followed by AuNps (B). Immobilization step with thiolated DNA probe (C). Plasma-enriched HIV RNA (D). Monitoring of hybridization through the current change of the anionic redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- by DPV (E).
FT-IR (A) and UV-Vis (B) spectra of cys-GQDs. Inset: cys-GQDs photographs under visible light and UV irradiation conditions (λex=365 nm).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms with anionic redox probe: bare CSPE (I), CSPE/cys-GQDs (II) electrode, and CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps electrode (III). Inset: histograms of the electrical charge involved in the oxidative and reductive processes. Electrolyte: [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (5.0 mmol/L) solution containing KCl (0.10 mol/L), v = 50 mV/s. (B) Cyclic voltammograms with phosphate buffer: bare CSPE (I), CSPE/cys-GQDs (II) electrode, and CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps electrode (III). Electrolyte: phosphate buffer (pH 7.4;0.10 mol/L), v = 30 mV/s. (C) Nyquist plots from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in KCl solution (0.1 mol/L) containing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (5 mmol/L) for bare CSPE (■),CSPE/cys-GQDs (•) and CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps electrode (▲). EIS conducted in open circuit, frequency ranging from 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz and amplitude of 10 mV. Full lines represent fitting from equivalent circuit simulation. Inset: bar chart of RCT from EIS of bare CSPE, CSPE/cys-GQDs and CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps and amplification of the Nyquist plots. (D) SEM images of bare CSPE (I), CSPE/cys-GQDs (II) and CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps (III).

(A) DPV of the anionic redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- in the presence of HIV probe, complementary target (HIV) and non-complementary (Zika and SARS-COV-2).) (B) Bar chart of the values of the current peaks with probe, positive target (HIV) and negative targets (Zika and SARS-COV-2). Electrolyte: [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (5.0 mmol/L) solution containing KCl (0.10 mol/L), modulation amplitude: 25 mV, pulse interval: 0.2 s, v = 50 mV/s. (C) AFM images of the modified electrode CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNPs with probe (I), HIV positive target (II) and SARS-COV-2 negative target (III).

(A) Calibration curve of the RNA HIV genosensor. (B) Differential pulse voltammograms of the redox probe with different target concentrations. (C) Bar chart of peak current (Ip) for RNA samples from people living with HIV (1−4) and sample of people who tested negative for HIV (5,6) (D) Storage stability at room temperature. Electrolyte: [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (5.0 mmol/L) solution containing KCl (0.10 mol/L), modulation amplitude: 25 mV, pulse interval: 0.2 s, v = 50 mV/s. For the calibration curve P value < 0.05.
Recent examples of GQDs-based electrochemical genosensors and aptasensors.LD: limit of detection; DLR: detection linear range.

head.ce:title: Fentogram electrochemical detection of HIV RNA based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Adolfo Lutz Institute, Regional Laboratory, 15061-020 São José do Rio Preto, São Paulo, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Uberlândia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Uberlândia, 38400–902, Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the construction of an HIV-specific genosensor through the modification of carbon screen-printed electrodes (CSPE) with graphene quantum dots decorated with L-cysteine and gold nanoparticles (cys-GQDs/AuNps). Cys-GQDs were characterized by FT-IR and UV–vis spectra and electronic properties of the modified electrodes were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The modification of the electrode surface with cys-GQDs and AuNps increased the electrochemical performance of the electrode, improving the electron transfer of the anionic redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- on the electrochemical platform. When compared to the bare surface, the modified electrode showed a 1.7 times increase in effective electrode area and a 29 times decrease in charge transfer resistance. The genosensor response was performed by differential pulse voltammetry, monitoring the current response of the anionic redox probe, confirmed with real genomic RNA samples, making it possible to detect 1 fg/mL. In addition, the genosensor maintained its response for 60 days at room temperature. This new genosensor platform for early detection of HIV, based on the modification of the electrode surface with cys-GQDs and AuNps, discriminates between HIV-negative and positive samples, showing a low detection limit, as well as good specificity and stability, which are relevant properties for commercial application of biosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: The first cases related to HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) infection occurred in 1981, yet 40 years have passed and the virus responsible for causing Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS) continues to be a global concern [1]. According to UNAIDS (Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS), since the beginning of the HIV pandemic, 85.6 million people have been infected with HIV. In 2022 it was estimated that 39 million people live with HIV, and 1.3 million were infected worldwide [2].

Early detection and diagnosis of HIV is critical for the rapid initiation of antiretroviral therapy (ART) [3]. The detection of HIV-related biomarkers depends on the time after the initial infection; viral RNA appears approximately 10 days after infection; afterward, the p24 antigen is detectable, however it is rapidly reduced due to the complexation of this antigen with emerging antibodies; about 4 to 6 weeks after the initial infection, antibodies begin to appear, and most infected individuals are seroconverted by 3 months [4]. Serological tests detect antigens and antibodies in serum mainly through enzyme-linked immunoenzymatic tests (EI), such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) testing. Molecular tests are used for the detection of RNA and DNA, such as reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) [5]. ELISA-based and RT-PCR tests are considered the gold standard in HIV diagnosis, yet both have limitations. For instance, the need for specialized people, the use of robust equipment, several stages in execution, large volumes of reagents and high cost rates [6].

Analytical methods are essential to ensure reliability in studies and processes that require safety, as well as tracking diseases and parameters involving quality of life [7]. Among the analytical methods, electroanalytical is the fastest, easiest, most selective, and most inexpensive [7]. Electrochemical biosensors are electroanalytical devices that use biomolecules to recognize a target analyte in biological or environmental samples, among others, which makes the analytical method more specific and selective [8–14].

Biosensors are analytical devices used to measure biological or chemical reactions by generating a signal proportional to the concentration of the analyte of interest [8]. The types of signal transducers are typically classified as optical, electrochemical and mass-based [9]. Biosensors with genetic material as a biological recognition element are genosensors [9]. These biological elements are usually small fragments of oligonucleotides, referred to as probes. These probes interact with DNA or RNA via the pairing of nitrogen bases, known as hybridization [10]. Hybridization is highly specific and causes changes in electrical parameters in the electrochemical platforms and generates signals that can be detected using electrochemical techniques, such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) [11].

Electrochemical platforms stand out for their low cost, the possibility of miniaturization, selectivity and stability, mainly when carbon inks are used to construct these platforms [11,12]. Carbon inks are non-metallic conductive agents that provide low detection limits. Conductive inks allow greater flexibility in electrode design and ease of application in screen printed electrodes (SPE) [11]. Therefore, all these features make carbon ink-based electrochemical biosensors market-friendly and promising in point-of-care diagnosis.

Nanomaterials are applied to increase the sensitivity of biosensor platforms due to unique characteristics such as size, electrical conductivity, plasmonic and quantum effects [6]. Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNps) were used to increase biosensor performance. GQDs are nanomaterials of the graphene family with zero dimension [6]. These materials are graphene sheets of less than 100 nm and have quantum effects, and extraordinary optical and electrical properties [13]. The high surface area of GQDs allows the anchoring of many molecules [14]. In addition, quantum confinement and edge effects lead to rapid electron transport and excellent conductivity, making these materials ideal for signal transduction [13,14].

AuNps are extensively studied due to their characteristics, such as catalytic, biological, optical, electrochemical and electronic activities [15]. The interaction between analyte and AuNps may alter physicochemical properties like surface plasmonic resonance, conductivity and redox behavior; all these changes produce detectable signals [16]. Furthermore, AuNps are easily conjugated with biomolecules for the recognition of antibodies and oligonucleotides and are widely explored in strategies for the immobilization of molecules in biosensors [16].

The standard methods for detecting biomarkers of HIV infection are expensive, laborious, and require large equipment and a large volume of reagents. Electrochemical biosensors are potential candidates for these criteria due to low cost, ease of mass production, sensitivity and selectivity [6,11].

In this work, a novel nanomaterial, GQDs functionalized with L-cysteine and AuNps, was produced and added onto a carbon screen-printed electrode previously developed by our group [17]. This novel platform enabled the development of a high-performance biosensor for HIV RNA detection. The genosensor showed sensitivity, specificity, and optimal shelf life.

Sulfuric acid (99%), gold (III) chloride trihydrate (≥ 99.9%), graphite flakes (> 99.9%) potassium ferricyanide (≥ 99.5%), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (≥99%), monosodium hydrogen phosphate (≥ 98%), sodium citrate dihydrate and potassium chloride (≥ 99%) were purchased from Neon (Brazil). Butyl glycol acetate (> 98%), vinyl resin (A.P), carbon black, silver nitrate (A.P), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) K3[Fe(CN6)] (>99%), potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) K4[Fe(CN)6] (> 99.5), methyl isobutyl ketone (99%), L-cysteine (A.P) and ethylene glycol (A.P) were purchased from Dinâmica (Brazil), Mexichem S/A (Mexico), Arinos (Brazil), Qhemis (USA), Fluka Analytical (USA), Acros Organics (USA), Synth (Brazil) and Vetec (Brazil), respectively.

The oligonucleotides used in this work were obtained from Exxtend (Brazil) and prepared in a phosphate buffer (pH7.4; 0.1 mol/L). The HIV1S probe was synthesized with thiol (5′-(SH)TGGTCTTCTGGGGCTTGTTCCAT-3′) and the non-complementary targets of SARS-COV-2 (5′-AATGTTTGTAACCGGCGTTT- 3′) and Zika virus (5′-CTTGTGTGTTGGGCAGCGG-3′) were used for the study of specificity. The specificity assays of the genosensor were conducted using gRNA (genomic RNA) from plasma samples from HIV+ and HIV- patients provided by Adolf Lutz Institute (SP). All the gRNA samples were purified using an ABBOTT m2000sp extractor (USA) and quantified by RT-PCR and spectrophotometer (BioDrop®, UK) prior to electrochemical detection. The use of human samples in this work is regulated by the Human Research Ethics Committee with the registration number 4348672.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were performed in CHI Instruments potentiostat (Model 760 C, USA) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in an Autolab potentiostat (Model PGSTAT302N, Metrohm, Switzerland). The CSPE was assembled and connected to the potentiostat via a screen-printed electrode adapter from DropSens (Spain). Infrared spectra of cysGQDs (graphene quantum dots functionalized with cysteine) were performed on the Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer (Fourier-Transform Infrared spectroscopy) (Perkin Elmer, USA) and morphological analyses were performed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) on a microscope from AFM5100N Hitachi (Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on an EVO MA10 Zeiss microscope (Germany). All analyses, represented as the arithmetic mean value ± standard deviation for triplicate measurements and unpaired t-test, were carried out when applicable using graphpad. All voltammograms were subtracted from their respective baseline. The value for the variation of peak current oxidation (Δip) was measured as the peak height.

The preparation of the inks and the construction of the electrode was carried out according to [17]. The carbon ink was printed as a working electrode and a counter electrode. Silver ink was used as a reference electrode (Agº). The inks were printed on PVC sheets using an adhesive mold and dried at 55 ºC for 25 min.

The synthesis of AuNps was performed following the Turkevich-Frens methodology [18]. This synthesis uses sodium citrate 1% (w/v) as a reducing agent of gold salts using tetrachloroauric acid 1% (w/v). Citrate ions stabilize the resulting nanoparticles. The AuNps were stored at 4 °C until their use.The absorption spectrum in UV-Vis was analyzed in a spectrophotometer using a 1:1 dilution in deionized water in quartz cuvettes (1 cm optical path). This solution has an absorption peak at 520 nm and refers to AuNps from 15 to 20 nm in size.

The synthesis of GQDs was performed by pyrolysis in a one-step method using ethylene glycol as a carbon source [19]. In an Erlenmeyer, 0.3 g of L-cysteine with 10 mL ethylene glycol was added. The solution was kept under agitation at 100ºC for 1 h. Initially, the solution is colorless and then develops into an orange and, lastly, turns into a dark yellow. The absorption spectrum in UV-Vis was analyzed in the spectrophotometer using a 1:1 dilution in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Genosensor assembly (CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps/HIV1S) and electrochemical detection of HIV RNA
ce:section.ce:para: All voltammetric parameters were tested prior to the experiments for the best analytical signal with the target.

Before use, all electrodes were subjected to CV in sulfuric acid (0.5 mol/L) solution and phosphate buffer (pH 7.4; 0.1 mol/L) between 0.00 V to + 1.00 V, 20 cycles at 500 and 100 mV/s. The electropolishing procedure removes impurities from the electrode surface and selects the ones with the same electroactive profile for the following experiments.

The genosensor construction steps are described in Fig. 1. The modification of the CSPE surface with cys-GQDs and AuNps was performed by drop casting. First, 3 µL of cys-GQDs was pipetted on the electrode surface and dried at 60ºC (Fig. 1A). Then, 3 µL of AuNps were pipetted and dried at 60ºC (Fig. 1B). 3 µL of thiolate probe HIV1S (10 µg/mL in phosphate buffer 0.1 mol/L) was dripped on the electrode and kept for 1 h at room temperature (Fig. 1C). After this period, the electrodes were washed in a phosphate buffer for 6 s

The gRNA samples from the patients were enriched with healthy plasma (1:1, v/v), prior to electrodetection, and then 3 µL (10 µg/mL) of gRNA-enriched plasma was pipetted on the electrode and left in a humid chamber at 55 ºC (Fig. 1D). After 20 min, the electrodes were washed in a phosphate buffer for 6 s and proceeded to detection by DPV. DPV was conducted (Fig. 1E) before and after the hybridization (modulation amplitude: 25 mV, pulse interval: 0.2 s, 50 mV/s) using the anionic redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (5 mmol/L), in KCl solution (0.1 mol/L) between − 0.40 V and + 0.80 V.

The genosensor calibration curve was constructed by varying the concentration of gRNA samples at 10 µg/mL, 1 µg/mL, 10 ng/mL, 1 pg/mL and 1 fg/mL. The genosensor was evaluated using 4 HIV+ and 2 HIV- patient samples, previously detected and quantified by RT-PCR. The stability of the genosensor was evaluated after storage at room temperature for 60 days, using probe-modified electrodes (CSPE/cys-GQDs/AuNps/HIV1S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of cys-GQDs by FT-IR and UV–vis spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR was used to characterize the functional groups of cys-GQDs. In Fig. 2A, the bands at 3321, 2961, 1636, 1038, 621 and 576 cm−1 can be observed. These bands can be attributed, respectively, to stretch vibration of the hydroxyl group (O-H), alkane (C-H), alkene (CC), amine (C-N) and sulfide (C-S) [19–22].

These results show that cys-GQDs have many functional groups that contribute to the binding sites for gold nanoparticles. UV–vis spectroscopy analysis (Fig. 2B) shows a peak at 350 nm and a shoulder at 225 nm. The shoulder can be attributed to the π to π transition of CC bonds in graphene sheets and the peak to the n to π transition of CO bonds in cys-GQDs [22,23] Photographs of the aqueous solutions of cys-GQDs are presented in the inset of Fig. 2B and show the yellow coloration and strong blue fluorescence under UV light, a fundamental characteristic of QDs.

One of the mechanisms that could be involved in forming cys-GQDs [21] is the generation of amide bonds between the carbon source and the cysteine amines in solution. In this case, the N-doped GQDs are formed in two steps, one involving the self-assembly of sheets to form graphene by dehydration and the other with the reaction of amides with neighboring carboxylic groups to form pyrrolic N through another molecular dehydration.

Electron transfer analysis at the electrode-solution interface was performed with the redox pair [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. Fig. 3 A shows a progressive increase in the anodic and cathodic peak currents of CSPE/cys-GQDs and CSPE/cys-GQDs, when compared to CSPE, meaning that electron transfer of the redox pair occurs more easily. The electric charge (Fig. 3 A – insert) increased 2 times after modification with cys-GQDS (0.13 ± 0004 mC) and 2.7 times after modification with cys-GQDs/AuNps (0.17 ± 0002 mC), compared to bare electrode (0.06 ± 0002 mC). The anodic peak current values (Ipa) for the unmodified electrode and for the electrode modified with cys-GQDs or with cys-GQDs/AuNps were 86.79 ± 1.64, 110.24 ± 0.68, and 139.06 ± 7.41 µA, respectively. Thus, the modification of the electrode surface with carbon quantum dots, as well as the interaction of this nanomaterial with gold nanoparticles caused a progressive improvement in electron transfer kinetics of the redox pair [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-.

The CV profiles (Fig. 3B) in phosphate buffer of the CSPE/cys-GQDs shows that the current increases (30.35 ± 0.85 µA) in relation to the unmodified electrode (0.37 ± 0.1 µA). This significant increase in conductivity is also observed in the low charge transfer resistance (Rct) in the EIS (Fig. 3 C); Rct decreases 5.5 times and 29 times when the bare CSPE (1.77 ± 0.06 kΩ) is compared with the electrode modified with CSPE/cys-GQDs (0.32 ± 0.02 kΩ) or modified with CSPE/cys-GQDs /AuNps (0.06 ± 0.004 kΩ). The increase in the electrode's capacitance is possibly due to the accumulation of charge at the electrode surface, which is accompanied by a decrease in resistance to load transfer, allowing the electronic exchange to occur more quickly. Due to its quantum confinement property, GQD can trap electrons and increase electronic sensitivity, promoting electron transfer toward the electrode surface [24]. These data show the efficiency of cys-GQDs and AuNps as electron transporters which causes a greater interaction between the electrode and analyte and increases the electrochemically active surface area [14].

The electrochemical activity and calculation of the dynamic surface area of the electrode were studied using the Randles-Sevcik Eq. (1) in phosphate buffer and ferrocyanide/ferricyanide redox pair [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. The equation implies that when there is an increase in scan rate, there is also an increase in current. At a higher rate, the current is higher, hindering the diffusion time of counter-ions in solution, and at a lower rate, there is better ionic transport at the electrode-solution interface. The linearities of the Ipa vs. v1/2 graph indicates that electron transfer occurs by free diffusion.
(1) Ipa = (2·69×105) n3/2 AD1/2 v 1/2 C

where n is the number of electrons involved in the reaction (1), A is the effective area of the electrode, D is the diffusion coefficient (7.17 × 10−6 cm2/s), v is the scan rate, and C is the concentration of electrolyte (5 mmol/L).

The effective area of the electrode was 0.0070 cm2, and the electrode with cys-GQDs-Au was 0.012 cm2. The increase in the effective area of the electrode reflects an increase in the amount of charge that modifies the reaction kinetics and improves electron transfer, reducing resistance and increasing the conductivity of the platform.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images show an increase in the homogeneity of globular structures in each modification step (Fig. 3D). In the electrode only with carbon ink (Fig. 3D-I), there are more cracks and globular structures, while the addition of cys-GQDs (Fig. 3D-II) and AuNps (Fig. 3D-III) promotes a smoothing of the surface.

In the development of high performance biosensors it is crucial to explore materials that amplify the signal, allowing the tracking of low amounts of analyte. The interaction between carbon ink and cys-GQDs can occur via π-π stacking. The interaction between cys-GQDS and AuNps probably occurs mainly via a covalent bond between gold and sulfur. However, since AuNps are stabilized by citrate, negative charges may interact electrostatically with positive nitrogen charges [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization and Detection of the HIV RNA
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to increasing the surface area of the modified electrode (CSPE/cys-GQDs), AuNps allows anchoring of the probe biomolecule HIV1S probe via the Au-S bond. The nitrogen bases of the probe may also interact with gold, but the Au-S bond occurs preferably, which ensures the orientation of DNA in the AuNps through the 5′-SH end of the probe [26].

The DPV profile of the anionic redox probe in the presence and absence of the complementary target are shown in Fig. 4 A. When the complementary target (HIV) is added, there is a reduction of the oxidation peak current (Ip) of 2.2x (from 97.8 ± 6.8 µA to 43.9 ± 2.9 µA, +0.13 V, Fig. 4B) in relation to the HIV probe. The current values of the negative targets (SARS-COV-2 and Zika virus) are respectively 84.62 ± 2.6 µA and 85.12 ± 2.1 µA, similar current values to the probe, showing the specificity of the genosensor. The specificity assay was carried out with viruses that present viral RNA as genetic material, such as HIV, and the choice was to analyze a possible false positive. The peak current value of the negatives was close to the value of the platform with the probe alone, suggesting that there was no significant interaction with the HIV probe. Furthermore, the selectivity test to negative patient samples showed current values close to the probe values. When comparing the mean peak current value of the probe with the HIV target in the paired t-test, the p-value was less than 0.001 (indicated with *** in Fig. 4B). In addition, the p-value to Zika virus and COVID target was 0.508, and 0.18446, respectively. This suggests that the current values are very similar and not significant and that the negative target is probably removed in the wash because of non-pairing with the specific probe.

The electrons transfer between the anionic redox probe and the electrode surface is hindered when the biorecognition occurs between the DNA probe and the positive target, as hybridization leads to the formation of double-strand DNA, which is less flexible than the single-strand DNA, causing a decrease in electron transport and consequent reduction of the peak current of the anionic redox probe [27].

Morphological changes onto the surface of the electrodes with the addition of different targets can be observed by AFM, (Fig. 4 C) as well as changes in the root mean square roughness coefficient (Rq). The electrodes with DNA probe (Fig. 4C-I), HIV positive target (Fig. 4C-II) and SARS-COV-2 negative target (Fig. 4C-III) presented Rq of 135.6 ± 18.3, 158.7 ± 13.6 and 129.6 ± 15.6 nm, respectively. After hybridization with the probe, roughness increases because of the specific interaction between the probe and HIV RNA and globular structures are formed, as double strand nucleic acids are stiffer and less flexible than single-strand DNA [27]. On the other hand, the roughness of the negative target surface was similar to the probe. Thus, the AFM analysis corroborates the electrochemical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve, storage stability and analysis with biological samples for HIV
ce:section.ce:para: The current response profile of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- with different concentrations of gRNA is shown in Fig. 5B. The lower the concentration of the complementary target, the higher the response, since fewer hybridization events occur and allows the redox probe to reach the electrode more easily. Thus, there was a logarithmic relationship Ip (µA) = 2.80 (µA/g/mL) x -log gRNA (g/mL) + 29.8, r = 0.99) between the concentration of gRNA and the current peak (Fig. 5 A) ranging from 10 µg/mL to 1 fg/mL. The obtained P value (< 0.05) was statistically significant.

The calibration curve was constructed using the concentration in µg/mL of total gRNA. It was possible to observe that the Ip values are in the range of the average peak current at 44 µA relative to 10 µg/mL. Pearson correlation analysis was performed between the variables of the two methods and showed a positive correlation with r = 0.52.

Tests with the biosensor were carried out with 4 RNA samples from people living with HIV (HIV +) and 2 negative samples (HIV -). All samples were diluted with healthy plasma (1:1). As shown in Fig. 5 C, all positive samples differed from the negative samples.

The stability of the response of biosensors to time is a critical parameter for future commercial use, since it refers to shelf life. The present genosensor maintained 100% of the response for 60 days (Fig. 5D), stored at room temperature and in closed containers. Most forms of storage involve the use of cooling selection (4ºC) [28–31], consequently adding a step that makes the maintenance of biosensors more costly. Table 1 lists other genosensors that also utilized GQD in the assembly, along with their analytical performance and stability. Some studies do not report stability tests, and some cases up to a couple of weeks or days.

To date, there are no genosensors in the literature for detecting HIV RNA based on GQDs as an electrochemical platform. Gogola et al. [31] developed an aptasensor for HIV p24 protein detection with a detection limit of 51.7 pg/mL. They used GQDs synthesized by citric acid pyrolysis as platform signal amplifiers. Kaimal et al. [28] recently developed a genosensor using the GQD interface to detect the guanine and adenine nitrogen bases with detection limits of 1.71 and 1.84 μmol/L, respectively. Most recent biosensors that use GQDs on electrochemical platforms using DNA as a biorecognition mode are aptasensors that mainly detect proteins and chemical compounds (Table 1). The HIV RNA is an essential biomarker for infection diagnosis and monitoring, and developing biosensor platforms that are selective, specific, and stable is crucial for point-of-care use.

The introduction of new nanomaterials is an important advance for the development of biosensor platforms. This study showed the application of GQDs functionalized with L-cysteine and AuNps in carbon ink electrodes and the remarkable effect on conductivity, where the synthesis of GQDs was performed in a simple way in a one-step method. Electrodetection was performed by DPV, monitoring the peak oxidation current of anionic redox probe K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. The genosensor showed low detection limit (1 fg/mL), and specificity, maintaining its response for 60 days at room temperature.

This study introduces a novel genosensor designed for detection of HIV RNA, based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles as electrochemical signal amplifiers. The proposed genosensor exhibits market potential due to its cost-effectiveness, utilization of affordable reagents, incorporation of ready-to-use electrodes, a one-step way for detection of viral RNA. and a simple operational procedure.

Moço Anna C. R: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. Gomide José A. L.: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Brussasco Jéssica G.: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Flauzino José M: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Gabriel luiz F: Validation, Formal analysis. Soares Márcia M. C. N.: Validation, Formal analysis. Madurro Joao M: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Brito-Madurro Ana G: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

R. Moço.
